objectives.current_and_planned_objectives:
1. Life Safety:
   - Conduct a complete primary and secondary search of all accessible areas of the Riverbend Logistics chemical warehouse and adjacent offices to confirm that all employees, contractors, and first responders are accounted for.
   - Establish and maintain a hot, warm, and cold zone with clear access control points to prevent unprotected personnel from entering contaminated or structurally compromised areas.
   - Ensure all responders operating in the hot and warm zones are using appropriate Level B or higher PPE, including SCBA, splash protection, and chemical-resistant gloves and boots, based on the current hazmat tech assessment.

2. Incident Stabilization:
   - Confine and suppress the active warehouse fire on the south side of the structure to prevent extension into the bulk storage area containing corrosive liquids and oxidizers (notably sodium hypochlorite and hydrogen peroxide solutions) as identified on the facility’s Tier II reports.
   - Prevent runoff of contaminated fire water into the adjacent Riverbend Creek by constructing and maintaining diking and damming operations at the facility’s south drainage ditch and installing absorbent booms and underflow dams at two downstream control points.
   - Coordinate with the utility company (Riverbend Municipal Utilities) to verify complete shutdown of gas and electricity to the affected structure while maintaining power to critical incident command and staging areas.

3. Environmental and Public Health Protection:
   - Maintain an initial 0.5-mile evacuation zone downwind of the incident, with shelter-in-place orders for the Riverview Apartments complex and the Willow Lane neighborhood until air monitoring confirms that airborne contaminant levels are below established health-based thresholds.
   - Continuously monitor air quality using multi-gas meters and photoionization detectors (PIDs) at the perimeter of the hot zone, at residential interface points, and at the Riverbend Elementary School campus to track VOCs, chlorine, and other potential irritants.
   - Coordinate with the county health department and state environmental quality agency to establish sampling plans for surface water, soil, and potential residue on nearby properties, documenting locations, times, and conditions.

4. Property Conservation:
   - Protect undamaged sections of the warehouse, especially the north office wing and adjacent small-business storage units, by proactive exposure protection, salvage covers, and controlled ventilation tactics.
   - Work with facility management to identify high-value inventory and critical records (e.g., servers, paper manifests, safety data sheet binders) and remove or shield them from heat, smoke, and water damage when safe to do so.

5. Coordination, Communications, and Documentation:
   - Establish a unified command structure including Fire, Law Enforcement, EMS, and the County HazMat Team, with a designated liaison to Riverbend Logistics management and the county emergency management office.
   - Implement a clear radio communications plan with designated tactical channels for suppression, hazmat operations, medical, and law enforcement, and ensure all incoming mutual-aid units are briefed on call signs and channel assignments.
   - Begin detailed incident documentation, including ICS forms, unit logs, photographs, and mapping of impacted areas, to support potential future investigation, claims, regulatory reporting, and after-action review.

6. Operational Timeframe Objectives (Next 4–8 Hours):
   - Achieve full knockdown of visible fire in the primary warehouse storage area within the next 2 hours while preventing fire spread to the northwest annex.
   - Complete secondary search of all accessible interior compartments and confirm all personnel are accounted for within 90 minutes.
   - Reduce airborne irritant levels at the residential interface to below action thresholds (as determined by HazMat Group) within 4 hours, allowing for phased lifting of shelter-in-place orders as appropriate.
   - Transition from purely defensive to targeted offensive overhaul operations in safe sectors of the building, guided by structural engineer or building official assessment, within 6 hours.

7. Planning for Next Operational Period (Beyond 8 Hours):
   - Develop a demobilization and turnover plan for the site, including coordination with private cleanup contractors, environmental consultants, and insurance representatives.
   - Establish a schedule and structure for a multi-agency after-action review and debriefing, with particular attention to industrial facility pre-plans, Tier II data access, and public notification processes.

actions_strategies_and_tactics.current_and_planned_actions_strategies_a:
Current Actions (First 2–3 Hours):
- Fire Attack and Containment:
  • Engine 3 and Engine 7 are positioned on the south and east sides of the warehouse, respectively, operating in a primarily defensive posture due to partial roof collapse over the central storage area. Master streams and large-caliber handlines are being used to cool the structure and prevent fire spread toward the bulk chemical storage racks on the west side.
  • Ladder 1 is set up on the southeast corner, providing elevated master stream coverage and serving as an observation platform for the Operations Section Chief to monitor fire behavior and structural integrity.

- HazMat and Air Monitoring:
  • County HazMat Team 2 has established a hazmat operations corridor on the east side of the building, with a formal entry control point and a technical decontamination line using portable pools and brush-down procedures.
  • HazMat technicians are conducting limited reconnaissance into the warm zone to identify breached containers, leaking drums, and any active chemical reactions, using multi-gas meters, PIDs, and colorimetric tubes.
  • Real-time air monitoring is underway at four perimeter points (north, south, east, west) and at the Riverview Apartments complex to track VOCs and potential chlorine or other irritant gases.

- Evacuation, Traffic, and Public Safety:
  • Riverbend Police Department has established roadblocks on Maple Avenue, 3rd Street, and the Riverbend Parkway on-ramps, redirecting traffic away from the industrial park and residential areas downwind of the incident.
  • Door-to-door notifications have been completed for the immediate half-mile downwind zone, with evacuation of the most at-risk residences closest to the facility and shelter-in-place instructions for those slightly further out.
  • A temporary evacuation center has been set up at Riverbend Community Center (1120 Oak Street), staffed by Red Cross volunteers and county emergency management personnel.

- Utilities and Infrastructure:
  • Riverbend Municipal Utilities has confirmed gas and electric service to the affected warehouse has been fully shut off. Power remains active to the command post and surrounding streets to support lighting and communications.
  • Public Works crews are placing sand and soil berms around storm drains and drainage ditches to contain contaminated runoff. Portable light towers are being deployed at the command post, staging area, and primary access points.

- Medical and Rehab:
  • EMS has established a medical and rehab sector in the cold zone near the staging area. All crews exiting the hot and warm zones are undergoing medical checks, including vital signs, CO monitoring, and hydration assessment.
  • One warehouse employee with smoke inhalation and minor burns has been transported to Riverbend General Hospital; two firefighters with heat exhaustion are being treated on scene and monitored for potential transport.

Planned Actions (Next 4–8 Hours):
- Transition from full defensive to targeted interior operations in structurally stable sectors, based on evaluation by the building official and safety officer.
- Systematically identify and isolate damaged chemical containers, with priority given to incompatible or reactive chemicals (oxidizers, acids, and organics) and those with higher acute toxicity.
- Implement product control measures (overpacking drums, plugging and patching, absorbent application) to stop active leaks and stabilize storage areas where feasible.
- Expand decontamination capabilities if the number of exposed responders or civilians increases, including setting up a separate gross decon line for ambulatory civilians, if needed.
- Coordinate with state environmental quality staff to begin initial surface water and soil sampling around Riverbend Creek and downstream drainage paths.
- Develop a rotation and relief plan for fire suppression and hazmat crews to prevent fatigue, including establishing a 2-on/1-off cycle for interior-capable personnel.
- Prepare for transition to an extended operations posture, including establishing a night operations plan with reduced but adequate staffing, perimeter security, and ongoing air monitoring.
actions_strategies_and_tactics.actions:
03:42 – First-arriving unit (Engine 3) reports heavy smoke showing from the south loading dock area; initiates size-up and requests second alarm and HazMat response.
03:47 – Battalion 1 assumes Incident Command, establishes Riverbend Command, and designates Maple Avenue and 5th Street intersection as the Incident Command Post (ICP).
03:55 – Second alarm assignment arrives; defensive fire attack begins with 2½-inch handlines and deck gun operations on the south side.
04:02 – Partial roof collapse observed over central warehouse bay; Safety Officer designates interior as no-go in that sector; operations remain defensive there.
04:10 – HazMat Team 2 arrives, begins establishing hazmat group, entry corridor, and technical decon line.
04:18 – Riverbend Police establish road closures and initial evacuation perimeter; public alert message issued via Reverse 911 and text alerts.
04:30 – Riverbend Municipal Utilities confirms shutoff of gas and electric to the affected structure; Public Works begins storm drain protection and runoff containment.
04:45 – Unified Command established with Fire, Law, EMS, and County Emergency Management; liaison assigned to coordinate with Riverbend Logistics management.
05:15 – Air monitoring stations established at four perimeter points and Riverview Apartments; no immediately dangerous levels detected outside hot/warm zones, but shelter-in-place remains in effect.
06:00 – Fire knockdown achieved in the south loading dock area; ongoing suppression focused on central storage racks and preventing extension to bulk chemical storage.
Planned (06:00–10:00):
- Conduct systematic secondary search in structurally sound office wing and peripheral storage bays.
- Begin targeted interior overhaul operations in areas cleared by Safety and building official.
- Initiate environmental sampling with state partners and refine evacuation/shelter-in-place zones based on updated air monitoring data.

prepared_by.prepared_by_position_title: Operations Section Chief, Riverbend Fire Department
